text: to choose the paths that I should follow. (Descartes, Discourse on Method) The general break with the mediaeval world has often been described as an ‘anthropological revolution’, placing man as the origin of meaning. This revolution occurred over several centuries affecting all the sub-domains of human thought and activity, and reformulating most basically the role of man in the production of truth, order and beauty. Breaking the ‘cosmological shell’ of the mediaeval universe implied a new emphasis on human creativity and human agency, and opened up new horizons of another man-made world. In philosophy this anthropological revolution can be identified as reaching its apex with Descartes and the Cartesian cogito, which on the standard view is seen as the beginning of modern philosophy, if not indeed of modern thought (Sorell 1997: xxix). If so, modern philosophy and thought originate with a very specific self-discovery that needs to be analysed in its own terms. One strong desire cuts across Descartes’ authorship: the need to undo a philosophy based on knowledge inherited from the past. We should not rely on ‘what others have thought’, as stated the third rule in Rules for the direction of the mind (Descartes 1997: 8). Descartes was here taking issue with the ancients, in particular with the scholastic tradition, but in general Descartes attacked customs and habits of the mind that people employed without use of proof-testing. He wanted to establish a method that could eliminate human errors from the chain of logical reasoning. In Discourse on Method (published in 1637), Descartes presented his scientific method, which was further developed in Meditations on First Philosophy, first published in Latin in 1641. Descartes’ philosophical trajectory can itself be analysed as a ritual passage. This is as implicitly argued in the short, suggestive Introduction to Descartes’ work by Chávez-Arvizo who likens Descartes’ metaphysical project to a ‘voyage’ of truth discovery undertaken by the nameless and timeless meditator (1997: xv). As stressed by van Gennep (1960) each step of the ritual passage (separation, liminality proper and re-integration) is subdivided into several rituals that test and ultimately transform the initiand who goes through the trial. The first stage of separation most often happens physically as the initiands are physically removed from their society, literally losing all hitherto existing reference points and prior knowledge. The tripartite structure is most clearly present in Meditations on First Philosophy. In the First Meditation Descartes starts his mental voyage by separating himself from all pre-established ‘opinions’; the method of doubt progressively takes apart all preconceived knowledge, starting by fundamentally doubting sense-based beliefs, doubting whether it is even possible to know whether one is asleep or awake, arriving at the utmost point of radical Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 121 doubt about the world: the heavens, the earth, the sounds and all ‘external things’ may be nothing but an illusion. In the Second Meditation Descartes overcomes the void and finds ‘something which is certain’, the cogito: the fact that I am thinking, therefore I exist. This new-gained certainty is immediately tested against further doubts. Descartes’ procedure here closely resembles the importance in ritual passages of proving one’s new identity through testing. The following meditations aim at testing the existence of God (the Third Meditation) and of material things (the Fifth Meditation). The Sixth Meditation establishes another truth, namely the distinction between mind and body, which is first tested and then established as a foundation that supports the cogito as belonging to the immortal mind. Descartes manages to come out at the other end of the passage with a newborn identity: a new Archimedean point of truth and knowledge, the fact that he is a thinking thing. He faces the entire gamut of un-truths that thrive within his world, and drives out the evil. The ritual passage is an individual voyage, but with universal implications: Descartes is simply laying bare a truth which is inbuilt in the universe, and kindly offers truth to the world. In fact, Descartes is both the initiand and the master of ceremony in the ritual passage. He establishes universal reason discovered in his individual self. When taken as a singular eruption of thought this sounds terribly like the idea of a madman, but the exercise had historical predecessors and a larger social field of emergence. Descartes and the Demon of Error Descartes’ bearing on the Christian atmosphere of seventeenth century Europe are very clear, and have been pointed out in the biographical literature. One major indirect impact was the general ‘internalization of religion’, produced by the Reformation and the Counter-Reformation (Gaukroger 1995: 25). The rival churches converged in the attempt to transform the church from public to private, to make faith a matter of personal internalized conviction. One of the tools was the generalized use of the ascetic technique of self-retreat, combined with the negative definition of the self as it had developed in the monasteries and as formulated in the idea of contemptus mundi that preached the hatred of the body and the world, the pervasiveness of sin and the fleetingness of time (Gaukroger 1995: 25). Descartes applied in converted form some of the ascetic techniques and contemptus mundi to himself and to his philosophy: he engaged in his own kind of therapeutic self-reform similar to the monastic experience; he hated the fluctuating truths of his perceptions, the weaknesses of his body, the un-truths which guided the world, the pervasiveness of error; and he had a sharp sense of the fleetingness of conceptual thought. Descartes’ own relationship with the church was full of tensions. Although he remained a confessional Catholic, he dismissed most of scholasticism, and attacked the Aristotelian philosophical premises that were then ruling Catholicism. This brought him into trouble with the Catholic Church, and therefore partly with his Liminality and the Modern 122 own Jesuit educational background. Descartes would constantly have to assure his Jesuit brothers that his rationalization of the world was not in contradiction with the teachings of the Church. Yet, as a resident in the Netherlands
